subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Particle Motion — $x(t) = e^{-t}\sin t$
stimulus: A particle moves along the $x$-axis with position at time $t$ given by $x(t) = e^{-t}\sin t$ for $0 \le t \le 2\pi$.

parts.prompt: Find the time $t$ at which the particle is farthest to the left. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: v(t) = x'(t) = e^{-t}(cos t - sin t). Set v(t) = 0: cos t = sin t, so t = pi/4, 5pi/4 on [0,2pi]. Check sign changes: v changes from + to - at t=pi/4 (right to left) and from - to + at t=5pi/4 (left to right). The leftmost point is at t=5pi/4 where x(5pi/4) is minimum. Must justify using first derivative test.

scoring_points.description: Correct derivative $x'(t) = e^{-t}(\cos t - \sin t)$ using product rule

alternatives.correct_example: $x'(t) = -e^{-t}\sin t + e^{-t}\cos t = e^{-t}(\cos t - \sin t)$
$e^{-t}(\cos t - \sin t) = 0 \Rightarrow \cos t = \sin t \Rightarrow t = \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{5\pi}{4}$ on $(0, 2\pi)$

scoring_points.description: Sign analysis or value comparison showing minimum (farthest left) at $t = \frac{5\pi}{4}$

alternatives.correct_example: $x'$ changes from negative to positive at $t = \frac{5\pi}{4}$, so $x$ has a relative minimum there

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion: particle is farthest to the left at $t = \frac{5\pi}{4}$ with justification (also check endpoints)

required_elements: Confirms global minimum by comparing $x(\frac{5\pi}{4})$ with endpoints $x(0) = 0$ and $x(2\pi) = 0$

common_traps: Not checking endpoints to confirm global minimum
Identifying $t = \pi/4$ (which is the farthest right) instead of $t = 5\pi/4$

parts.prompt: Find the value of the constant $A$ for which $x(t)$ satisfies the equation $Ax''(t) + x'(t) + x(t) = 0$ for $0 < t < 2\pi$.
parts.scoring_notes: x(t) = e^{-t}sin t; x'(t) = e^{-t}(cos t - sin t); x''(t) = e^{-t}(-sin t - cos t) + e^{-t}(cos t - (-sin t))... compute carefully: x''(t) = d/dt[e^{-t}(cos t - sin t)] = -e^{-t}(cos t - sin t) + e^{-t}(-sin t - cos t) = e^{-t}(-cos t + sin t - sin t - cos t) = e^{-t}(-2cos t) = -2e^{-t}cos t. Now substitute: A*(-2e^{-t}cos t) + e^{-t}(cos t - sin t) + e^{-t}sin t = 0. Divide by e^{-t}: -2A cos t + cos t - sin t + sin t = 0 => (1 - 2A)cos t = 0 for all t => 1 - 2A = 0 => A = 1/2.

alternatives.correct_example: $x''(t) = -e^{-t}(\cos t - \sin t) + e^{-t}(-\sin t - \cos t) = -2e^{-t}\cos t$

scoring_points.description: Substitutes $x$, $x'$, $x''$ into the equation $Ax'' + x' + x = 0$

required_elements: Substitutes all three expressions into the ODE
alternatives.correct_example: $A(-2e^{-t}\cos t) + e^{-t}(\cos t - \sin t) + e^{-t}\sin t = 0$

common_traps: Error in computing $x''(t)$ leading to wrong $A$
